title: Newton polygons of smooth curves
speaker: Elena Mantovan
abstract:
The Schottky problem is a classical question asking for a characterization of Jacobians among abelian varieties.
In positive characteristic, one may asking whether there are values of discreet invariants known to occurs for abelian varieties which do not occur for Jacobians.
In this talk, I will focus on one such invariant, the Newton polygon, and discuss an approach to this question which relies on the study of the geometry of Shimura varieties intersecting the Torelli locus.
This talk is based on joint work with Li and Singh.

proof_stages.label: Torelli-Newton polygon problem for Jacobians
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the problem of which Newton polygons of principally polarized abelian varieties occur on the Torelli locus in positive characteristic, reviews the Newton stratification of A_g, and explains the dimension obstruction and Oort-style alternatives for M_g.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to cyclic covers and PEL Shimura subvarieties
proof_stages.summary: To obtain a more rigid setting, the speaker fixes abelian—mainly cyclic—covers of P^1, computes the induced multiplication type on Jacobians, and factors the family through a PEL-type Shimura subvariety of A_g.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-side Newton stratification and special-family benchmark
proof_stages.summary: Known theorems on PEL Shimura varieties give a computable nonempty Newton stratification with closure and dimension formulas, while special cyclic/abelian families where the curve locus equals the Shimura variety provide a benchmark in which all allowable strata occur.

proof_stages.label: Nonemptiness theorems for cyclic-cover curve loci
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Bouw’s maximal p-rank result, the speaker states joint results proving nonemptiness of the μ-ordinary locus for cyclic covers with r≤5 under a lower bound on p, and nonemptiness of a non-μ-ordinary or codimension-one EO locus under stronger bounds.

proof_stages.label: Prime-dependent alternatives in a nonspecial family
proof_stages.summary: A one-dimensional nonspecial m=7 family is analyzed to show that, depending on p mod 7, the curve locus can exhibit all strata, skip an intermediate stratum, or have basic points only sporadically, so no homogeneous closure rule should be expected.

proof_stages.label: Pullback of the μ-ordinary Hasse invariant
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy uses the existence of an automorphic Hasse invariant whose vanishing cuts out the non-μ-ordinary locus on the Shimura variety, and translates nonemptiness questions into explicit nonvanishing and vanishing statements for its pullback to the Hurwitz parameter space.

proof_stages.label: Partial Hasse invariants and EO refinements
proof_stages.summary: Factoring the Hasse invariant into partial Hasse invariants refines the Newton problem to EO strata, so codimension-one loci are detected by zeros of individual factors and basic loci by common zeros, with the splitting behavior of p governing how many factors are distinct.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic computation and limitations
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion explains how EO-type algorithms for curves and polynomial coefficient computations make the criteria effective, while also emphasizing that the method is sporadic and does not yet reverse-engineer families for arbitrary desired polygons or nonabelian quotients.

examples.name: Low-genus Torelli cases
examples.note: The cases g≤3 are cited as the well-understood situation where the Torelli image is open, motivating the focus on g≥4 rather than supplying any step of the later argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only states that for genus up to 3 the Torelli image is an open embedding and the question is well understood

passes.reason: The genus ≤3 Torelli cases are the low-end/base cases where the Torelli image has atypically full/open behavior, used only as a well-understood contrast.

The cases g≤3 are the threshold/boundary Torelli cases where the image still has full open dimension before the higher-genus codimension problem begins.

examples.name: Ordinary and supersingular Newton polygons
examples.note: The slopes 0,1 and the all-1/2 polygon make the definition, symmetry, and partial order on Newton polygons concrete.

passes.reason: Ordinary and supersingular Newton polygons are the two endpoint strata in the Newton polygon poset.

ordinary and supersingular are the maximally regular endpoint Newton polygons in the partial order, introduced as standard examples
The ordinary and supersingular Newton polygons are the opposite extreme strata, with maximally regular endpoint/central slope patterns.

ordinary and supersingular Newton polygons are the canonical lowest and highest highly regular endpoints of the Newton polygon poset.
ordinary and supersingular polygons are the lowest/open and highest/closed Newton-polygon extremes, not counterexamples
Endpoint Newton polygons—the ordinary open minimum and supersingular closed maximum—with maximally regular slope patterns.
The ordinary and supersingular Newton polygons are the two endpoint strata of the Newton polygon order, not obstructions to a claim.

examples.name: Three-branch-point cyclic covers
examples.note: The r=3 case is a zero-dimensional toy case whose Jacobians are CM abelian varieties, illustrating why the positive-dimensional cases r≥4 are the interesting ones.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the r=3 cyclic-cover case by computing the moduli dimension as zero, identifying the Jacobian as CM, and explaining that its Newton polygon is computable from Shimura’s formula

passes.reason: Three-branch-point cyclic covers are rigid zero-dimensional/minimal-branch cases with CM Jacobians, used as a toy computable case.
Three branch points make the cyclic-cover Hurwitz space rigid and zero-dimensional, the minimal stable branch-point boundary.
with three branch points the cyclic-cover moduli collapses to a single CM point, the minimal branch-number toy case
Three branch points form the rigid minimal branch case, so the parameter space collapses to dimension zero and the Jacobian is CM.
Three branch points give the rigid zero-dimensional minimal branch-point cyclic-cover case.
With exactly three branch points the cyclic-cover family is the minimal rigid zero-dimensional case, giving a CM Jacobian with computable Newton polygon.

examples.name: The m=7 cyclic cover with monodromy (3,2,1,1)
examples.note: This one-dimensional nonspecial family is used repeatedly as a concrete test case displaying prime-dependent Newton polygons, the possible Oort alternatives, and the partial-Hasse/common-root computation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the m=7, monodromy (3,2,1,1) family, giving congruence-class-dependent Newton polygons, signatures, dimensions, and stratum behavior

examples.name: Bouw’s nonabelian cover with unattained maximal p-rank
examples.note: This counterexample is presented as evidence that the Shimura-variety dictionary and the abelian-cover hypotheses cannot be naively extended to nonabelian covers.
